content:
# Joshua Chapter 14 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 237-238 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_014_page_001.jpg`

1. And these are what the Sons of Israel inherited in the land of Canaan, which Eleazar the priest and Joshua the son of Nun and the heads of the fathers of the tribes of the Sons of Israel gave as an inheritance to them —

2. by the lot of their inheritance, as YHWH had commanded by the hand of Moses, for the nine tribes and the half-tribe.

3. For Moses had given the inheritance of the two tribes and the half-tribe on the other side of the Jordan; but to the Levites he had given no inheritance among them.

4. For the sons of Joseph were two tribes — Manasseh and Ephraim — and they gave no portion to the Levites in the land, except cities to dwell in, with their pasturelands for their livestock and for their possessions.

5. As YHWH had commanded Moses, so the Sons of Israel did, and they divided the land.

6. Then the sons of Judah drew near to Joshua at Gilgal, and Caleb the son of Jephunneh the Kenizzite said to him: "You know the word that YHWH spoke to Moses the man of God concerning me and concerning you at Kadesh-barnea.

7. I was forty years old when Moses the servant of YHWH sent me from Kadesh-barnea to spy out the land, and I brought back word to him as was in my heart.

8. But my brothers who went up with me made the heart of the people melt, and I wholly followed YHWH my God.

9. And Moses swore on that day, saying: 'Surely the land upon which your foot has trodden shall be an inheritance to you and to your sons forever, because you have wholly followed YHWH my God.'

10. And now, behold, YHWH has kept me alive, as He spoke, these forty-five years since YHWH spoke this word to Moses, when Israel walked in the wilderness. And now, behold, I am today eighty-five years old.

11. I am still today as strong as I was on the day Moses sent me; as my strength was then, so is my strength now, for war and for going out and for coming in.

12. And now, give me this hill country of which YHWH spoke on that day, for you heard on that day that the Anakim are there, and that the cities are great and fortified. Perhaps YHWH will be with me, and I shall drive them out, as YHWH spoke."

13. And Joshua blessed him, and he gave Hebron to Caleb the son of Jephunneh as an inheritance.

14. Therefore Hebron became the inheritance of Caleb the son of Jephunneh the Kenizzite to this day, because he wholly followed YHWH, the God of Israel.

15. And the name of Hebron formerly was Kiriath-arba — he was the great man among the Anakim. And the land had rest from war.

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **YHWH (יהוה):** Rendered consistently as "YHWH" throughout, preserving the Tetragrammaton as it appears in the Leningrad Codex. The divine name occurs nine times in this chapter (vv. 2, 6, 8, 9 [twice], 10 [twice], 12, 14), underscoring that the entire land distribution is understood as YHWH's covenantal act.

2. **"Sons of Israel" (בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל):** Rendered literally per LOGOS convention (vv. 1, 5) rather than the interpretive "Israelites" or gender-neutralized "children of Israel."

3. **"heads of the fathers" (רָאשֵׁי אֲבוֹת):** The Hebrew in v. 1 reads *rashei avot ha-mattot*, literally "heads of the fathers of the tribes." This preserves the patriarchal clan structure reflected in the Hebrew text rather than smoothing it to "family heads" or "clan leaders."

4. **"by the lot" (בְּגוֹרָל, be-goral):** The lot-casting in v. 2 was not random chance but a recognized means of discerning YHWH's will (cf. Proverbs 16:33). The formal rendering preserves the instrumental *beth* preposition — "by the lot" rather than "by casting lots."

5. **"pasturelands" (מִגְרְשֵׁיהֶם, migrsheihem):** In v. 4, the Hebrew *migrash* refers to open land surrounding Levitical cities designated for grazing. Rendered "pasturelands" rather than "suburbs" (KJV) or "open lands" to convey the specific agricultural function.

6. **"the Kenizzite" (הַקְּנִזִּי, ha-Qenizzi):** Caleb's gentillic designation (vv. 6, 14) identifies him as belonging to the Kenizzite clan. This is significant: the Kenizzites were originally a non-Israelite people (cf. Genesis 15:19), and Caleb's inclusion in Israel's inheritance demonstrates that covenant faithfulness, not ethnic origin, determined one's place among YHWH's people.

7. **"man of God" (אִישׁ הָאֱלֹהִים, ish ha-Elohim):** Moses' title in v. 6. This designation appears here and is distinct from "servant of YHWH" (*eved YHWH*) used elsewhere for Moses (cf. Joshua 1:1). The title emphasizes Moses' prophetic authority as the mediator through whom YHWH's word came.

8. **"wholly followed" (מִלֵּא אַחֲרֵי, mille acharei):** Literally "filled after" — the Piel of *male* with *acharei*. This distinctive phrase occurs four times in the chapter (vv. 8, 9, 14, and implied in v. 12), forming the thematic backbone of Caleb's claim. The idiom conveys complete, undivided loyalty — Caleb "filled up" his following of YHWH with no remainder. Rendered "wholly followed" to preserve this intensity.

9. **"made the heart of the people melt" (הִמְסִיו אֶת־לֵב הָעָם, himsiu et-lev ha-am):** In v. 8, the Hiphil of *masas* — to cause to melt, dissolve. The same root describes the Canaanites' fear of Israel in Joshua 2:11. Here it is Israel's own heart that was melted by the faithless spies' report. The formal rendering preserves the visceral Hebrew metaphor.

10. **"Kiriath-arba" (קִרְיַת אַרְבַּע):** In v. 15, the ancient name of Hebron. The Hebrew means "City of Arba" (or "City of Four"). The text identifies Arba as *ha-adam ha-gadol ba-Anaqim* — "the great man among the Anakim." The syntax is ambiguous: *adam* could mean "man" specifically or "person" generically. Given the proper-name context and the formal register, "the great man" is retained.

11. **"forty-five years" / "eighty-five years old" (vv. 10–11):** Caleb's arithmetic establishes the narrative timeline: he was forty at Kadesh-barnea (v. 7), and forty-five years have since passed (v. 10), making him eighty-five (v. 10b). This places the distribution at Gilgal approximately seven years after the Jordan crossing, since Israel wandered thirty-eight years after Kadesh-barnea (Deuteronomy 2:14) before entering the land.

12. **"Perhaps YHWH will be with me" (אוּלַי יהוה אוֹתִי):** The particle *ulai* in v. 12 expresses hopeful contingency, not doubt. Caleb is not uncertain of YHWH's power but acknowledges with reverence that divine aid is a gift, not a presumption. This is the language of faith that defers to YHWH's sovereign will while acting boldly.

13. **"And the land had rest from war" (וְהָאָרֶץ שָׁקְטָה מִמִּלְחָמָה):** The closing formula of v. 15 employs *shaqat*, "to be quiet, to rest" — a key term in Joshua that marks the fulfillment of YHWH's promise of rest (cf. Joshua 1:13, 15; 11:23; 21:44; 23:1). This refrain frames the entire land-distribution section.

14. **Structural note:** Joshua 14:1–5 serves as the preamble to the western tribal allotments (chapters 14–19), establishing the authority (Eleazar, Joshua, tribal heads), method (lot), and scope (nine and a half tribes). Caleb's individual grant in vv. 6–15 precedes the tribal allotments, honoring his singular faithfulness with first place in the distribution narrative.

— LOGOS
